passage:
How Teenagers Get Their News
A recent survey conducted among 1,200 high school students in the UK found that 68% of teenagers get their daily news from social media platforms such as Instagram and TikTok. Only 24% said they regularly watch television news, and fewer than 10% read printed newspapers.
Many teenagers explain that social media is faster and more convenient. Short videos and headlines allow them to understand key information in just a few minutes. In addition, algorithms recommend content based on personal interests, making news consumption more engaging.
However, media researchers warn that this trend may create “information bubbles.” Because users mostly see content that matches their opinions, they may not be exposed to different viewpoints. Moreover, false or misleading information can spread quickly when users share posts without checking reliable sources.
Schools are now introducing media literacy lessons to teach students how to evaluate online information. Experts believe that young people should learn to compare different news sources, check publication dates, and identify trustworthy websites. With proper guidance, social media can become a useful tool rather than a source of misinformation.

questions.question: 23. The word “interests” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _____.
24. The word “misleading” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____.

25. Which of the following best paraphrases the sentence:
“With proper guidance, social media can become a useful tool rather than a source of misinformation.”
answers: Social media is always a reliable source of information.
If used correctly, social media can be helpful instead of spreading false information.
Social media should replace traditional news completely.
People should stop using social media to avoid misinformation.

questions.question: 26. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage?
answers: The percentage of teenagers reading printed newspapers.
The influence of parents on teenagers’ news habits.
The introduction of media literacy lessons at schools.

questions.question: 27. In which paragraph does the writer mention the danger of being exposed to only similar viewpoints?
28. In which paragraph does the writer present survey statistics about teenagers’ news habits?
29. The word “them” in paragraph 2 refers to _____.
30. Which of the following statements is TRUE according to the passage?
answers: Most teenagers prefer printed newspapers to social media.
Less than one-fourth of teenagers regularly watch television news.
Social media never spreads false information.
Schools discourage students from using social media.

passage:
AI AND THE FUTURE OF WORK
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming the global job market. A recent report by economic analysts suggests that by 2030, automation and AI could replace up to 30% of repetitive tasks currently performed by humans. While some fear that this will lead to mass unemployment, many experts argue that technology will instead create new categories of jobs that do not exist today.
The primary advantage of AI in the workplace is its ability to handle 'boring' or dangerous tasks. In manufacturing and data entry, AI systems work faster and with higher precision than humans. This allows employees to focus on more creative and complex projects. Furthermore, AI tools can analyze vast amounts of data in seconds, helping managers make better business decisions and increasing overall productivity.
However, the transition to an AI-driven economy presents significant challenges. Workers in traditional sectors may find their skills becoming obsolete. For instance, if a company automates its customer service with AI chatbots, human staff may lose their positions. Moreover, there is a growing 'digital divide' where only those with advanced technical skills can access high-paying roles, leaving others behind.
To address these issues, many governments and organizations are emphasizing 'upskilling' and 'reskilling.' Educational programs are being redesigned to teach students how to work alongside AI rather than compete with it. Experts suggest that soft skills, such as empathy, leadership, and critical thinking, will become more valuable because AI cannot easily replicate them. With the right approach, AI can be a partner that enhances human potential.

questions.question: 31. The word “vast” in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to _____.
32. The word “obsolete” in paragraph 3 is OPPOSITE in meaning to _____.

33. Which of the following best paraphrases the sentence:
“Educational programs are being redesigned to teach students how to work alongside AI rather than compete with it.”
answers: Students are being taught to stop using AI in schools.
Schools are changing their lessons so students can use AI as a tool instead of a rival.
AI will soon replace all teachers and educational programs.
Students must learn how to build AI systems to get a job.

questions.question: 34. Which of the following is NOT mentioned in the passage?
answers: The percentage of tasks that might be automated by 2030.
The benefits of AI in handling dangerous or repetitive work.
The specific names of companies that have fired workers due to AI.
The importance of soft skills like empathy and leadership.

questions.question: 35. In which paragraph does the writer mention the potential for new job categories to be created?
36. In which paragraph does the writer discuss the risk of certain workers losing their jobs?
37. The word “them” in paragraph 4 refers to _____.
38. Which of the following statements is TRUE according to the passage?
answers: AI is slower than humans when performing data entry tasks.
All experts believe AI will definitely cause permanent mass unemployment.
Soft skills are expected to be more important in an AI-driven world.
AI can easily replicate human empathy and critical thinking.
